examples.en: Maria is as intelligent as João.

examples.pt: Este livro é tão bom como aquele.
examples.en: This book is as good as that one.

rules.rule: Superlative: o/a mais + adjective + de.
rules.rulePt: Superlativo: o/a mais + adjetivo + de.

examples.pt: Ela é a mais alta da turma.
examples.en: She is the tallest in the class.

examples.pt: Ela é a mais simpática da turma.
examples.en: She's the nicest in the class.

examples.pt: Este é o restaurante mais caro de Lisboa.
examples.en: This is the most expensive restaurant in Lisbon.

rules.rule: Irregular: bom→melhor, mau→pior, grande→maior, pequeno→menor.
rules.rulePt: Irregulares: bom→melhor, mau→pior.

examples.pt: Este vinho é melhor do que aquele.
examples.en: This wine is better than that one.

examples.pt: A situação está pior do que ontem.
examples.en: The situation is worse than yesterday.

tips: Do que (not just 'que') after mais/menos in comparisons.
Melhor and pior don't change for gender: o melhor, a melhor.
tipsPt: Mais/menos + do que.
Melhor e pior não variam em género.

questions.questionText: Complete: Ela é mais alta ___ eu.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela é mais alta ___ eu.
questions.explanation: More than = mais...do que.
Bom → melhor (irregular).
As...as = tão...como.
The tallest = o mais alto de (group).
Mau → pior.

questions.questionText: Complete: Este livro é ___ do que esse.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Este livro é ___ do que esse.
questions.explanation: Less...than = menos...do que.

questions.questionText: Which is correct for 'the tallest in the class'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual está correto para 'the tallest in the class'?
questions.explanation: Superlative: a mais alta de (contraction da) turma.

questions.questionText: Complete: O café está ___ quente ___ o chá.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O café está ___ quente ___ o chá.
questions.explanation: Hotter than = mais quente do que.
Grande → maior (irregular).

questions.questionText: Complete: Ela é a ___ da família.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela é a ___ da família.
questions.explanation: The youngest = a mais nova (da família).
Pequeno → menor.

questions.questionText: Complete: Este exercício é ___ difícil ___ o outro.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Este exercício é ___ difícil ___ o outro.
questions.explanation: As difficult as = tão difícil como.
O melhor, a melhor — same form melhor.

questions.questionText: Complete: O João é ___ do que o Pedro.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O João é ___ do que o Pedro.
questions.explanation: Taller than = mais alto do que.
Bom → melhor. The best = o melhor.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Lisbon is more expensive than Porto'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Lisboa é mais cara do que o Porto'?
options: Lisboa é mais cara que Porto.
Lisboa é mais cara do que o Porto.
Lisboa é cara mais do que Porto.
Lisboa é tão cara como o Porto.
questions.correctAnswer: Lisboa é mais cara do que o Porto.
questions.explanation: More...than = mais + adjective + do que. 'Do que' is preferred in EP (just 'que' is also accepted).

questions.questionText: What is the comparative form of 'bom'?
questions.explanation: Bom has an irregular comparative: melhor (better), not *mais bom.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'as intelligent as'?
questions.explanation: As...as = tão + adjective + como.

questions.questionText: 'Este é o restaurante mais caro ___ Lisboa.' — fill in:
questions.questionTextPt: 'Este é o restaurante mais caro ___ Lisboa.'
questions.explanation: Superlatives use 'de' (not 'do que'): o mais caro DE Lisboa.

questions.questionText: What is the irregular comparative of 'mau'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o comparativo irregular de 'mau'?
questions.explanation: Mau → pior (worse). Like English bad → worse.

imperfect-formation.title: Imperfect Tense Formation
imperfect-formation.summary: -AR: falava/falávamos; -ER/-IR: comia/partia. Only 4 irregulars: ser→era, ter→tinha, vir→vinha, pôr→punha.
imperfect-formation.intro:
The imperfect is used for ongoing or habitual past actions and descriptions. -AR: falava, falavas, falava, falávamos, falavam. -ER: comia, comias, comia, comíamos, comiam.

-IR: partia, partias, partia, partíamos, partiam. Only 4 verbs are irregular in the imperfect: ser (era), ter (tinha), vir (vinha), pôr (punha). Much more regular than the preterite.

rules.rule: -AR imperfect: -ava, -avas, -ava, -ávamos, -avam.
rules.rulePt: Imperfeito -AR: -ava, -ávamos, -avam.

rules.rule: -ER/-IR imperfect: -ia, -ias, -ia, -íamos, -iam.
rules.rulePt: Imperfeito -ER/-IR: -ia, -íamos, -iam.

rules.rule: Irregular: ser→era, ter→tinha, vir→vinha, pôr→punha.
rules.rulePt: Irregulares: era, tinha, vinha, punha.

tips: Imperfect sets the scene; preterite tells what happened.
Only 4 irregulars in imperfect — easier than preterite!
tipsPt: O imperfeito descreve; o perfeito narra.
Só 4 irregulares no imperfeito.

questions.questionText: What is the eu form of 'falar' in the imperfect?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a forma 'eu' de 'falar' no imperfeito?
questions.explanation: -AR imperfect: falava.
Habitual past: imperfect comia.

questions.questionText: What is the imperfect of 'ser' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o imperfeito de 'ser' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Ser imperfect: era.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Nós ___ em Lisboa. (we used to live)
questions.explanation: -ER imperfect nós: vivíamos.
Ter imperfect: tinha.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela ___ sempre tarde. (used to arrive)
questions.explanation: -AR imperfect: chegava.

questions.questionText: What ending do -ER verbs use in imperfect for 'ele'?
questions.explanation: -ER imperfect: -ia (comia).

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: ___ muito em criança. (I used to play)
questions.explanation: Habitual: imperfect jogava.
Vir imperfect: vinha.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Os miúdos ___ na rua. (used to play)
questions.explanation: -AR imperfect eles: jogavam.

questions.questionText: What is the nós form of 'partir' in imperfect?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a forma 'nós' de 'partir' no imperfeito?
questions.explanation: -IR imperfect: partíamos.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O João ___ feliz. (was happy - description)
questions.explanation: Description in the past: era.
Pôr imperfect: punha.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Tu ___ sempre café. (you used to drink)
questions.explanation: -ER imperfect tu: bebias.

questions.questionText: How many verbs are irregular in the imperfect?
questions.questionTextPt: Quantos verbos são irregulares no imperfeito?
questions.explanation: Ser, ter, vir, pôr.

preterite-vs-imperfect.title: Preterite vs Imperfect
preterite-vs-imperfect.summary: Preterite: completed actions, specific moments. Imperfect: habitual past, descriptions, ongoing. Often used together.
preterite-vs-imperfect.intro:
Use the preterite for completed actions and specific moments in the past: Ontem comi peixe (Yesterday I ate fish). Use the imperfect for habitual past actions, descriptions, and ongoing states: Quando era criança, comia peixe todos os dias (When I was a child, I used to eat fish every day).

They often appear together: the imperfect sets the scene, the preterite interrupts: Eu dormia quando o telefone tocou (I was sleeping when the phone rang). Signal words: ontem, uma vez, de repente (preterite) vs sempre, normalmente, antigamente, enquanto (imperfect).

rules.rule: Preterite: completed actions, specific events.
rules.rulePt: Perfeito: ações completas, eventos específicos.

examples.en: He called at three o'clock. (specific time)
We bought a new car. (one-time event)

rules.rule: Imperfect: habitual past, descriptions, ongoing.
rules.rulePt: Imperfeito: hábitos, descrições, decorrer.

examples.en: When I was a child, I used to eat fish.

examples.pt: Quando era criança, comia pizza todos os sábados.
examples.en: When I was a child, I ate pizza every Saturday. (habitual)

examples.pt: O céu estava azul e o sol brilhava.
examples.en: The sky was blue and the sun was shining. (description)

examples.pt: Ela morava em Lisboa quando era jovem.
examples.en: She lived in Lisbon when she was young. (ongoing state)

rules.rule: Together: imperfect sets scene, preterite interrupts.
rules.rulePt: Juntos: imperfeito pinta o quadro, perfeito interrompe.

examples.pt: Eu dormia quando o telefone tocou.
examples.en: I was sleeping when the phone rang.
He called while I was sleeping. (preterite interrupts imperfect)

examples.pt: Estava a chover quando saí de casa.
examples.en: It was raining when I left the house.

tips: Ontem, uma vez, de repente → preterite. Sempre, enquanto, quando era → imperfect.
Background (was doing) = imperfect; what happened (did) = preterite.
tipsPt: Ontem, de repente → perfeito. Sempre, enquanto → imperfeito.
Cenário = imperfeito; evento = perfeito.

questions.explanation: Completed action: preterite.
Habitual past: imperfect for both.
Enquanto (while) = ongoing → imperfect.
Background (dormia) + event (tocou).

questions.questionText: Which is for a one-off event?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é para um evento único?
questions.explanation: One-off = preterite.

questions.questionText: Complete: ___ uma vez fui a Paris.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: ___ uma vez fui a Paris.
questions.explanation: Uma vez = once = preterite.
De repente = suddenly → preterite.

questions.questionText: Complete: O céu ___ azul e o sol ___.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O céu ___ azul e o sol ___. (description)
questions.explanation: Description of scene: imperfect.

questions.questionText: Which tense for 'I used to go'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual tempo para 'I used to go'?
questions.explanation: Habitual = imperfect.
While I was sleeping (imperfect), someone arrived (preterite).
Antigamente = in the old days → habitual/description.

questions.questionText: Complete: Ela ___ o livro quando eu ___.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela ___ o livro quando eu ___. (She was reading when I arrived)
questions.explanation: Was reading (background) = lia; I arrived (event) = cheguei.
Used to eat = comia (imperfect).
Suddenly = de repente → preterite.

questions.questionText: In 'Eu estudava quando ele chegou', which action is the background?
questions.questionTextPt: Em 'Eu estudava quando ele chegou', qual é o cenário?
questions.explanation: Estudava = was studying (background). Chegou = arrived (event).
Completed past action with 'ontem' → preterite: comi.

questions.questionText: 'Quando era criança, _____ pizza todos os sábados.' (I used to eat)
questions.questionTextPt: 'Quando era criança, _____ pizza todos os sábados.'
questions.explanation: Habitual past action → imperfect: comia.
Ongoing background action interrupted → imperfect: dormia. The interruption (telefonou) is preterite.

questions.questionText: Which tense is used for descriptions of past scenes?
questions.questionTextPt: Que tempo se usa para descrever cenas no passado?
questions.explanation: Scene-setting and descriptions use the imperfect: 'O céu estava azul e o sol brilhava.'

questions.questionText: '_____ a chover quando saí de casa.' (It was raining)
questions.questionTextPt: '_____ a chover quando saí de casa.'
questions.explanation: Ongoing situation → imperfect of estar: Estava a chover. The departure (saí) is preterite.

future-tense.title: Future Tense
future-tense.summary: Infinitive + ei, ás, á, emos, ão. Irregular stems: fazer→far-, dizer→dir-, trazer→trar-. In spoken EP 'ir + infinitive' is more common.
future-tense.intro:
The synthetic future is formed by adding endings to the infinitive: ei, ás, á, emos, ão (falar→falarei, falarás, falará, falaremos, falarão). Only 3 verbs have irregular stems: fazer→far- (farei), dizer→dir- (direi), trazer→trar- (trarei).

In European Portuguese spoken language, 'ir + infinitive' is more common for the near future: Vou falar com ele (I'm going to talk to him).

The synthetic future is used more in formal/written EP and for certainty or promises.

rules.rule: Future: infinitive + ei, ás, á, emos, ão.
rules.rulePt: Futuro: infinitivo + ei, ás, á, emos, ão.

examples.en: Could it be true? (future for conjecture)

rules.rule: Irregular stems: fazer→far-, dizer→dir-, trazer→trar-.
rules.rulePt: Irregulares: far-, dir-, trar-.

examples.en: I will tell the truth. (dizer → dir-)
They will bring the books. (trazer → trar-)
We will do our best. (fazer → far-)

tips: In spoken EP we usually say 'Vou falar' instead of 'Falarei'.
Falarei is more formal or for promises/certainty.
tipsPt: Em PE falado dizemos mais 'Vou falar'.
Falarei é mais formal.

questions.questionText: What is the eu form of 'falar' in the future?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a forma 'eu' de 'falar' no futuro?
questions.explanation: Future: infinitive + ei = falarei.
Both correct; vou falar more common in speech.

questions.questionText: What is the future stem of 'fazer'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o radical do futuro de 'fazer'?
questions.explanation: Fazer → far- (farei, farás, etc.).

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Nós ___ a verdade. (we will tell)
questions.explanation: Dizer future: dir- + emos = diremos.

questions.questionText: Which is more common in spoken EP for future?
questions.questionTextPt: O que é mais comum em PE falado para o futuro?
questions.explanation: Vou falar is more common in everyday EP.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Eu ___ o trabalho. (I will do)
questions.explanation: Fazer future: farei.

questions.questionText: What is the future of 'trazer' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o futuro de 'trazer' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Trazer → trar-: trarei.
Vir future: virá (regular from infinitive).

questions.questionText: Which verbs have irregular future stems?
questions.explanation: Only 3: fazer (far-), dizer (dir-), trazer (trar-).

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: ___ o que disse. (I will do)
questions.explanation: Farei = I will do.

questions.questionText: What ending does the future use for 'eles'?
questions.questionTextPt: Que desinência tem o futuro para 'eles'?
questions.explanation: Falarão, comerão — ão.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O João ___ o livro. (will bring)
questions.explanation: Trazer future: trará.

questions.questionText: When is the synthetic future (falarei) often used?
questions.questionTextPt: Quando se usa o futuro sintético?
questions.explanation: More in formal writing and for certainty.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Tu ___ com ele. (you will speak)
questions.explanation: Future tu: falarás.
Comer future: comeremos.

questions.questionText: In spoken EP, which construction replaces the simple future?
questions.questionTextPt: No PE falado, que construção substitui o futuro simples?
questions.explanation: In speech, 'Vou falar' (ir + infinitive) is far more common than 'Falarei' (simple future).

questions.questionText: 'Será verdade?' — what does the future express here?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Será verdade?' — o que exprime o futuro aqui?
questions.explanation: The future can express conjecture: 'Será verdade?' = Could it be true? / I wonder if it's true.

questions.questionText: What is the irregular future stem of 'dizer'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o radical irregular do futuro de 'dizer'?
questions.explanation: Dizer → dir-: direi, dirás, dirá, diremos, dirão.
Formal context → simple future: falará.

questions.questionText: Which register typically uses the simple future (falarei)?
questions.questionTextPt: Que registo usa tipicamente o futuro simples (falarei)?
questions.explanation: The simple future sounds formal/literary in EP. Casual speech uses ir + infinitive.

conditional-tense.title: Conditional Tense
conditional-tense.summary: Infinitive + ia, ias, ia, íamos, iam. Same irregular stems as future: faria, diria, traria. Uses: polite requests, hypotheticals.
conditional-tense.intro:
The conditional is formed by adding -ia, -ias, -ia, -íamos, -iam to the infinitive (or to the same irregular stem as the future). Falar→falaría, comer→comeria.

Same 3 irregulars: faria, diria, traria. Uses: polite requests (Poderia ajudar-me?), hypothetical situations (Eu viajaria mais se tivesse dinheiro), and advice (Eu no teu lugar, falaria com ele).

rules.rule: Conditional: infinitive + ia, ias, ia, íamos, iam.
rules.rulePt: Condicional: infinitivo + ia, íamos, iam.

examples.en: Could you help me, please? (polite request)
Would it be possible to change the reservation?

rules.rule: Same irregular stems as future: faria, diria, traria.
rules.rulePt: Mesmos radicais do futuro: faria, diria, traria.

examples.pt: Se tivesse dinheiro, viajaria pelo mundo.
examples.en: If I had money, I'd travel the world.
He said he would come tomorrow. (reported speech)
If I could, I'd stay longer.

tips: Poderia = could (polite). Queria = I would like. Conditional is often used with 'se' (if).
Faria = I would do; diria = I would say.
tipsPt: Poderia = poderia (pedido educado).
Condicional com 'se' (hipótese).

questions.questionText: What is the conditional of 'falar' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o condicional de 'falar' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Conditional: falaría (in EP often falaria).
Could you help me? = polite conditional.

questions.questionText: What does the conditional often express?
questions.explanation: Poderia? = could you? Viajaria se... = would travel if.

questions.questionText: Complete: Eu ___ mais se tivesse tempo.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Eu ___ mais se tivesse tempo.
questions.explanation: Hypothetical: I would travel more.

questions.questionText: What is the conditional stem of 'fazer'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o radical do condicional de 'fazer'?
questions.explanation: Same as future: far- → faria.

questions.questionText: Complete: ___ no teu lugar, ___ com ele.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: ___ no teu lugar, ___ com ele.
questions.explanation: In your place I would speak = Eu no teu lugar, falaria.
Dizer conditional: diria.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela ___ o livro se ___. (would bring / could)
questions.explanation: Conditional + imperfect subjunctive in 'if' clause.
Comer conditional: comeríamos.
I would like = Queria (or conditional queria).

questions.questionText: Which ending does conditional use for 'nós'?
questions.questionTextPt: Que desinência usa o condicional para 'nós'?
questions.explanation: Comeríamos, falaríamos.
Fazer conditional: farias.

questions.questionText: What is the conditional of 'trazer' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o condicional de 'trazer' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Trazer → trar-: traria.
Polite: Poderia ajudar?

questions.questionText: When do we use conditional with 'se'?
questions.explanation: Se tivesse... faria = If I had... I would do.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Could you help me?' politely?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Could you help me?' de forma educada?
questions.explanation: The conditional makes requests more polite: poderia (could/would be able to).

questions.questionText: 'Se tivesse dinheiro, _____ pelo mundo.' (I would travel)
questions.explanation: Hypothetical condition (se + imperfect subjunctive) → conditional: viajaria.

questions.questionText: 'Disse que _____ amanhã.' (he would come — reported speech)
questions.explanation: In reported speech, future becomes conditional: virá → viria.
Regular conditional: infinitive + -ia → gostaria.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'I would like a coffee'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'I would like a coffee'?
questions.explanation: Polite request using conditional: Gostaria de um café.

ir-plus-infinitive.title: Ir + Infinitive
ir-plus-infinitive.summary: Conjugated ir + infinitive = going to do. Most common way to express future in spoken EP.
ir-plus-infinitive.intro:
Ir + infinitive is the most common way to express the future in spoken European Portuguese. Formation: conjugated form of ir + infinitive.

Vou comer (I'm going to eat), Vamos viajar (We're going to travel). It can express immediate future (Vou sair agora) or planned future (Vamos viajar no verão). Compare with the synthetic future: Falarei is more formal/written; Vou falar is everyday speech.

rules.rule: ir (conjugated) + infinitive = future intention.
rules.rulePt: ir (conjugado) + infinitivo = intenção futura.

rules.rule: Ir + infinitive is the most common way to express future actions in spoken EP. It's much more natural than the simple future tense.
rules.rulePt: Ir + infinitivo é a forma mais natural de expressar o futuro em português falado.

examples.pt: Vou fazer compras depois do trabalho.
examples.en: I'm going to go shopping after work.
She's going to call you later.
We're going to travel in the summer.

rules.rule: In the negative, put 'não' before 'ir': Não vou comer isso. The infinitive always stays at the end.
rules.rulePt: Na negativa, 'não' vem antes de 'ir'.

examples.en: I'm not going to go out today.

examples.pt: Ele não vai conseguir chegar a tempo.
examples.en: He's not going to manage to arrive on time.

tips: In EP speech, 'Vou falar' is much more common than 'Falarei'.
Use it for plans and immediate future.
tipsPt: Em PE 'Vou falar' é muito mais comum que 'Falarei'.
Para planos e futuro próximo.

questions.questionText: How do you form 'going to' future in Portuguese?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se forma o futuro 'going to'?
questions.explanation: Vou + infinitive.
I'm going to leave now = Vou sair.

questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'we're going to travel'?
questions.explanation: Ir + infinitive: vamos viajar.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Ela ___ ao cinema. (is going to go)
questions.explanation: She's going to the cinema = Ela vai ao cinema. For 'going to go' we'd say vai ir or irá.

questions.questionText: Which is more common in spoken EP for future?
questions.questionTextPt: O que é mais comum em PE falado?
questions.explanation: Ir + infinitive is more common in speech.
Vou falar = I'm going to talk.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between 'Vou falar' and 'Falarei'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a diferença entre 'Vou falar' e 'Falarei'?
options: Vou falar = everyday; Falarei = more formal
questions.correctAnswer: Vou falar = everyday; Falarei = more formal
questions.explanation: Vou falar is colloquial; Falarei is formal/written.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Nós ___ no verão. (we're going to travel)
questions.explanation: Vamos + infinitive.
Vou sair agora = immediate. Vamos viajar no verão = planned.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Tu ___ amanhã? (are you going to come)
questions.explanation: Vais + vir = you're going to come.

questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'I'm going to eat'?
questions.explanation: Ir + comer = vou comer.
They're going to the beach = Vão à praia. (ir + a + place).

questions.questionText: Which sentence is typical in EP conversation?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual frase é típica em conversa em PE?
questions.explanation: Vou falar is everyday.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: ___ fazer o trabalho. (I'm going to do)
questions.explanation: Vou fazer = I'm going to do.

questions.questionText: Does 'Vamos viajar' need a future time reference?
questions.explanation: Context often makes it clear (we're going to travel).

subjunctive-triggers.title: Subjunctive Triggers
subjunctive-triggers.summary: After wishes (espero que), emotions (tenho medo que), doubt (talvez), necessity (é preciso que), and conjunctions (embora, para que). Different subject after 'que'.
subjunctive-triggers.intro:
The subjunctive is used after certain triggers. Wishes/desires: espero que, quero que, desejo que. Emotions: tenho medo que, fico feliz que, lamento que.

Doubt: duvido que, não acredito que, talvez. Necessity: é preciso que, é necessário que, é importante que. Conjunctions: embora (although), antes que (before), para que (so that), sem que (without), até que (until). Crucially, after 'que' when the subject of the two clauses is different: Quero que TU fales (I want YOU to speak) — subjunctive because the subject of 'falar' is different from 'eu'.

rules.rule: Wishes: espero que, quero que + subjunctive.
rules.rulePt: Desejos: espero que, quero que + conjuntivo.

examples.en: I want you to do this.

examples.pt: Espero que tenhas um bom dia.
examples.en: I hope you have a good day.
I wish them to be happy.

rules.rule: After 'que' when subjects are different.
rules.rulePt: Depois de 'que' quando os sujeitos são diferentes.

rules.rule: Doubt/uncertainty: talvez, duvido que + subjunctive.
rules.rulePt: Dúvida: talvez, duvido que + conjuntivo.

tips: If the subject of the verb after 'que' is different from the main clause, use subjunctive.
É preciso que, é importante que always take subjunctive.
tipsPt: Sujeito diferente depois de 'que' → conjuntivo.
É preciso que, é importante que → conjuntivo.

questions.questionText: After 'espero que', which mood do you use?
questions.questionTextPt: Depois de 'espero que' que modo se usa?
questions.explanation: Espero que + subjunctive.
Different subject (I want / you be) → subjunctive.

questions.questionText: Why do we use subjunctive in 'Quero que fales'?
questions.questionTextPt: Porque usamos conjuntivo em 'Quero que fales'?
questions.explanation: I want + you speak → different subjects.
Talvez + subjunctive.
Espero que = hope → subjunctive.
É preciso que + subjunctive.
Embora (although) + subjunctive.
Emotion + que + subjunctive.
Acho que + indicative (belief). Espero que + subjunctive.
Antes que + subjunctive.
Para que = so that + subjunctive.
Doubt/negation + que + subjunctive.

questions.questionText: Which conjunction means 'although' and takes subjunctive?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual conjunção significa 'although' e leva conjuntivo?
questions.explanation: Embora = although + subjunctive.
É importante que + subjunctive.

questions.questionText: In 'Quero que ele venha', why subjunctive?
questions.questionTextPt: Em 'Quero que ele venha', porque conjuntivo?
questions.explanation: I want / he come → different subjects.
Querer que triggers subjunctive: faças (not fazes).
Embora always triggers subjunctive: seja.

questions.questionText: Which emotion triggers the subjunctive after 'que'?
questions.questionTextPt: Que emoção exige conjuntivo depois de 'que'?
questions.explanation: Emotions (happy, sad, surprised) + que trigger subjunctive: 'Fico feliz que estejas bem.'
Duvidar que (doubt that) triggers subjunctive: venha.
Impersonal expressions (é importante que) trigger subjunctive: estudemos.

present-subjunctive-formation.title: Present Subjunctive Formation
present-subjunctive-formation.summary: Take eu form of present, drop -o, add -e/-es/-e/-emos/-em (-AR) or -a/-as/-a/-amos/-am (-ER/-IR). Irregulars: ser→seja, estar→esteja, ter→tenha.
present-subjunctive-formation.intro:
To form the present subjunctive: take the eu form of the present indicative, drop the -o, and add the subjunctive endings. -AR verbs: -e, -es, -e, -emos, -em (fale, fales, fale, falemos, falem).

-ER and -IR: -a, -as, -a, -amos, -am (coma, comas, coma, comamos, comam; parta, partas, parta, partamos, partam). Key irregulars: ser→seja, estar→esteja, ter→tenha, ir→vá, dar→dê, saber→saiba, haver→haja, querer→queira. Example: Espero que estejas bem (I hope you're well).

rules.rule: -AR subjunctive: -e, -es, -e, -emos, -em (from present eu -o).
rules.rulePt: Conjuntivo -AR: -e, -es, -e, -emos, -em.

rules.rule: -ER/-IR subjunctive: -a, -as, -a, -amos, -am.
rules.rulePt: Conjuntivo -ER/-IR: -a, -as, -a, -amos, -am.

rules.rule: Irregulars: ser→seja, estar→esteja, ter→tenha, ir→vá.
rules.rulePt: Irregulares: seja, esteja, tenha, vá.

examples.pt: Espero que tenhas um bom dia.
examples.en: I hope you have a good day. (ter → tenha)
It's better that you go now. (ir → vá)
I want you to know the truth. (saber → saiba)

examples.pt: Embora dê trabalho, vale a pena.
examples.en: Although it's hard work, it's worth it. (dar → dê)

examples.pt: Duvido que ele esteja em casa.
examples.en: I doubt he's at home. (estar → esteja)

tips: Subjunctive -AR has -e (not -a); -ER/-IR have -a (not -e). Opposite of indicative!
Esteja, tenha, vá, dê, saiba are very common.
tipsPt: -AR conjuntivo: -e. -ER/-IR: -a.
Esteja, tenha, vá são muito usados.

questions.questionText: What is the subjunctive of 'falar' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o conjuntivo de 'falar' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: -AR subjunctive: fale.
Estar subjunctive: estejas.

questions.questionText: What is the subjunctive of 'ser' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o conjuntivo de 'ser' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Ser subjunctive: seja.
Chegar -AR subjunctive: chegues.

questions.questionText: Which ending does -ER subjunctive use for 'ele'?
questions.questionTextPt: Que desinência usa o conjuntivo -ER para 'ele'?
questions.explanation: -ER subjunctive: -a (coma).
Dizer subjunctive: digas (irregular).

questions.questionText: What is the subjunctive of 'ter' for 'tu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o conjuntivo de 'ter' para 'tu'?
questions.explanation: Ter subjunctive: tenhas.
Vir subjunctive: venha.
-AR subjunctive nós: -emos → falemos.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: É importante que ___. (that we try)
questions.explanation: Tentar -AR subjunctive: tentemos.

questions.questionText: What is the subjunctive of 'ir' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o conjuntivo de 'ir' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Ir subjunctive: vá.
Saber subjunctive: saiba.
-IR subjunctive: parta.

questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Espero que ___. (that you're well - tu)
questions.explanation: Estar subjunctive tu: estejas.

questions.questionText: What is the subjunctive of 'dar' for 'eu'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o conjuntivo de 'dar' para 'eu'?
questions.explanation: Dar subjunctive: dê (with accent).

questions.questionText: What is the present subjunctive of 'ter' (eu)?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o presente do conjuntivo de 'ter' (eu)?
questions.explanation: Ter is irregular in the subjunctive: tenha, tenhas, tenha, tenhamos, tenham.

questions.questionText: What is the present subjunctive of 'ir' (eu)?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o presente do conjuntivo de 'ir' (eu)?
questions.explanation: Ir is irregular: vá, vás, vá, vamos, vão.

questions.questionText: How do you form the present subjunctive of regular -AR verbs?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se forma o presente do conjuntivo de verbos regulares em -AR?
questions.explanation: -AR verbs 'swap' to -e endings in the subjunctive: fale, fales, fale, falemos, falem.

questions.questionText: 'Espero que _____ um bom dia.' (you have — tu, ter)
questions.explanation: Esperar que triggers subjunctive: tenhas (not tens).

questions.questionText: What is the present subjunctive of 'saber' (eu)?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o presente do conjuntivo de 'saber' (eu)?
questions.explanation: Saber is irregular in the subjunctive: saiba, saibas, saiba, saibamos, saibam.

relative-pronouns.title: Relative Pronouns
relative-pronouns.summary: que (that/which/who), quem (who, after prepositions), onde (where), o qual/a qual (formal), cujo/cuja (whose).
relative-pronouns.intro:
Relative pronouns introduce relative clauses. Que is the most common — for people and things: O homem que vi (The man that I saw). Quem is only for people and is used after prepositions: A pessoa com quem falei (The person I spoke with).

Onde is for places: A cidade onde nasci (The city where I was born). O qual, a qual, os quais, as quais are more formal, often after prepositions: A razão pela qual parti (The reason why I left). Cujo/cuja/cujos/cujas mean 'whose': O homem cuja casa é grande (The man whose house is big).

rules.rule: que = who/that/which (people and things).
rules.rulePt: que = que/qual.

examples.pt: O homem que vive ali é o meu vizinho.
examples.en: The man who lives there is my neighbour.

examples.pt: O filme que vimos ontem era ótimo.
examples.en: The film that we saw yesterday was great.

rules.rule: quem = who, only for people, after prepositions.
rules.rulePt: quem = quem (pessoas, após preposição).

examples.pt: A pessoa com quem falei era simpática.
examples.en: The person I spoke with was nice.

examples.pt: O amigo a quem emprestei o livro devolveu-o.
examples.en: The friend to whom I lent the book returned it.

rules.rule: onde = where (places). cujo/cuja = whose.
rules.rulePt: onde = onde. cujo/cuja = cujo.

examples.pt: O homem cuja casa é grande.
examples.en: The man whose house is big.

examples.pt: A cidade onde nasci é pequena.
examples.en: The city where I was born is small.

examples.pt: O professor cujo livro li é famoso.
examples.en: The professor whose book I read is famous.

tips: Use 'que' for most cases. Use 'quem' after prepositions when referring to people.
O qual/a qual are formal; use when the preposition would make 'que' sound wrong (pela qual).
tipsPt: Que para a maioria. Quem após preposição (pessoas).
O qual é mais formal.

questions.questionText: Which relative pronoun is used for both people and things?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual pronome relativo serve para pessoas e coisas?
questions.explanation: Que = who/that/which.

questions.questionText: Complete: O homem ___ vi é alto.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O homem ___ vi é alto.
questions.explanation: The man (that) I saw: que.
A pessoa com quem falei.

questions.questionText: Complete: A cidade ___ nasci fica no norte.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: A cidade ___ nasci fica no norte.
questions.explanation: Place: onde = where.
Cuja casa = whose house.
With whom I spoke = com quem.

questions.questionText: Which is 'the reason why I left'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é 'the reason why I left'?
questions.explanation: Formal: pela qual (by which).

questions.questionText: Complete: O livro ___ comprei é bom.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O livro ___ comprei é bom.
questions.explanation: The book (that) I bought: que.
Onde = where (place).

questions.questionText: Complete: O homem ___ casa é grande.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: O homem ___ casa é grande.
questions.explanation: Whose house = cuja casa (casa is f.).
O qual, a qual are formal relatives.
The woman (that) I met: que.
Por + a qual = pela qual.

questions.questionText: Complete: A casa ___ moro é antiga.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: A casa ___ moro é antiga.
questions.explanation: Where I live = onde moro.
Cujo/cuja/cujos/cujas = whose.

questions.questionText: 'O homem _____ vive ali é o meu vizinho.' — which relative pronoun?
questions.questionTextPt: 'O homem _____ vive ali é o meu vizinho.'
questions.explanation: Que is the most common relative pronoun — it works for both people and things as subject or object.

questions.questionText: 'A pessoa com _____ falei era simpática.' — which pronoun?
questions.explanation: After prepositions referring to people, use quem: com quem, a quem, de quem.

questions.questionText: 'A cidade _____ nasci é pequena.' — which pronoun?
questions.explanation: Onde is used for places: a cidade onde nasci.

questions.questionText: 'O professor _____ livro li é famoso.' — which pronoun?
questions.explanation: Cujo means 'whose' — it connects a person to something they possess: cujo livro.

questions.questionText: Does 'cujo' agree with the possessor or the thing possessed?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Cujo' concorda com o possuidor ou a coisa possuída?
questions.explanation: Cujo agrees with the thing possessed: cujo livro (m.), cuja casa (f.), cujos filhos (m.pl.).

connectors.title: Conjunctions & Connectors
connectors.summary: Connecting ideas — although, while, since, as long as, so that — the glue that holds complex sentences together.
connectors.intro:
Conjunctions and connectors are the words that link ideas, clauses, and sentences together. Coordinating conjunctions (e, ou, mas) connect equal elements. Subordinating conjunctions (porque, embora, quando, se) create dependent clauses that add meaning to the main clause.

A key B1 skill is knowing which conjunctions trigger the subjunctive — embora, antes que, para que, caso, mesmo que all require it. Mastering these connectors transforms your Portuguese from simple sentences to complex, nuanced expression.

rules.rule: Cause: porque, como, já que, visto que.
rules.rulePt: Causa: porque, como, já que.

examples.en: I didn't go because I was ill.

examples.pt: Como estava a chover, ficámos em casa.
examples.en: Since it was raining, we stayed home.

examples.pt: Visto que não há alternativa, aceito.
examples.en: Given that there's no alternative, I accept.

rules.rule: Contrast: mas, porém, embora, apesar de, no entanto.
rules.rulePt: Contraste: mas, embora, apesar de.

examples.pt: É caro, mas vale a pena.

examples.en: Although I'm tired, I'll go out. (subjunctive!)
Despite it being expensive, I bought it.

examples.pt: Mesmo que chova, vou à praia.
examples.en: Even if it rains, I'll go to the beach. (subjunctive!)

rules.rule: Result: por isso, portanto, assim, então.
rules.rulePt: Resultado: por isso, portanto, assim.

examples.pt: Choveu. Por isso, fiquei em casa.
examples.en: It rained. So I stayed home.

examples.pt: Estudei muito, por isso passei no exame.
examples.en: I studied a lot, so I passed the exam.

examples.pt: Estava doente, então fiquei em casa.
examples.en: I was ill, so I stayed home.

rules.rule: Coordinating conjunctions: e (and), ou (or), mas (but), nem (neither/nor), portanto (therefore), porém/contudo (however).
rules.rulePt: Conjunções coordenativas: e, ou, mas, nem, portanto, porém, contudo.

examples.pt: Gosto de café mas prefiro chá.
examples.en: I like coffee but I prefer tea.

examples.pt: Choveu muito, portanto a estrada está molhada.
examples.en: It rained a lot, therefore the road is wet.

rules.rule: Time conjunctions: quando (when), enquanto (while), assim que (as soon as), desde que (since/ever since), até que (until — subjunctive).
rules.rulePt: Conjunções temporais: quando, enquanto, assim que, desde que, até que (conjuntivo).

examples.pt: Enquanto ele cozinha, eu ponho a mesa.
examples.en: While he cooks, I set the table.
As soon as you arrive, call me.

examples.pt: Desde que chegámos, não parou de chover.
examples.en: Since we arrived, it hasn't stopped raining.

tips: EMBORA is the #1 conjunction that trips up learners because it requires the subjunctive. 'Embora esteja' not *'embora está'.
'Como' at the start of a sentence means 'since/as'. In the middle, it usually means 'how' or 'like'.
Many conjunctions trigger the subjunctive: embora, antes que, até que, para que, caso, mesmo que. This is a key B1 skill.
Porém and contudo mean 'however' and are more formal than mas. No entanto is another alternative.
Por isso (therefore/so) vs porque (because): por isso gives the RESULT, porque gives the CAUSE.
tipsPt: Embora exige sempre o conjuntivo: 'Embora esteja cansado'.
'Como' no início da frase significa 'visto que/já que'.
Muitas conjunções exigem o conjuntivo: embora, antes que, até que, para que, caso.

questions.explanation: Porque = because.
Because = porque.
Por isso, portanto = therefore.
Result: Therefore I stayed.
Mas = but.
Although = embora + subjunctive.
Enquanto = while.

questions.questionText: Complete: Fiz isso ___ tu pudesses descansar.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Fiz isso ___ tu pudesses descansar.
questions.explanation: So that = para que + subjunctive.
Para = to/for (purpose).
When you arrive = quando chegar.
No entanto = however, nevertheless.

questions.questionText: Complete: Não só ___ português ___ também espanhol.
questions.questionTextPt: Completa: Não só ___ português ___ também espanhol.
questions.explanation: Not only...but also = não só...mas também.

questions.questionText: Which connector is for 'as soon as'?
questions.explanation: Logo que = as soon as.
Despite (the rain) = apesar de.
Já que = since (because).
Embora triggers the subjunctive: esteja, not está or estou.
Enquanto = while. Quando = when. Embora = although.

questions.questionText: 'Como estava a chover, ficámos em casa.' — what does 'como' mean here?
questions.explanation: 'Como' at the start of a sentence means 'since/as' (giving a reason).

questions.questionText: Which of these does NOT trigger the subjunctive?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual destas NÃO exige o conjuntivo?
questions.explanation: Porque (because) takes the indicative. Embora, antes que, and para que all trigger the subjunctive.
Nem = neither/nor. Used in negative sentences: não...nem.

opposites.title: Opposites & Word Formation
opposites.summary: How to form and use opposites in Portuguese — from prefix rules that let you decode unfamiliar words, to common antonym pairs every learner needs.
opposites.intro:
Portuguese has two main ways to express opposites. The first is using prefixes to transform a word into its opposite — much like English uses 'un-', 'in-', or 'dis-'.

The second is simply knowing the antonym pairs (grande/pequeno, quente/frio). Learning the prefix rules is especially powerful because it gives you a tool to understand new words you have never seen before. If you know 'feliz' means happy, you can immediately decode 'infeliz' as unhappy.

This topic covers both strategies.

rules.rule: The prefix 'in-' is the most common way to form adjective opposites. It works like English 'un-' or 'in-'. Before words starting with 'b', 'm', or 'p', it changes to 'im-'.
rules.rulePt: O prefixo 'in-' é a forma mais comum de formar opostos de adjetivos. Antes de palavras que começam com 'b', 'm' ou 'p', muda para 'im-'.
exceptions: Not all adjectives use 'in-'. Many common opposites are completely different words: bom/mau (good/bad), grande/pequeno (big/small).

rules.rule: The prefix 'des-' is used mainly with verbs and some adjectives. It expresses reversal or removal — like English 'un-' or 'dis-'. This is one of the most productive prefixes in Portuguese.
rules.rulePt: O prefixo 'des-' usa-se principalmente com verbos e alguns adjetivos. Expressa reversão ou remoção.

examples.en: to turn on / connect → to turn off / disconnect

exceptions: 'Despir' is irregular — it does not follow the pattern of simply adding 'des-' to 'vestir'. The stem changes.

rules.rule: The prefix 'i-' is used before words starting with 'l' or 'r'. Before 'l' the word gets 'il-', before 'r' it gets 'ir-'. This mirrors the English pattern exactly.
rules.rulePt: O prefixo 'i-' usa-se antes de palavras que começam com 'l' ou 'r'. Antes de 'l' usa-se 'il-', antes de 'r' usa-se 'ir-'.

rules.rule: Common adjective opposites that use completely different words — no prefix pattern. These must be memorised as pairs.
rules.rulePt: Opostos comuns de adjetivos que usam palavras completamente diferentes — sem padrão de prefixo. Devem ser memorizados como pares.
exceptions: 'Novo' means both 'new' and 'young'. The opposite of 'novo' (new) is 'velho' (old), and the opposite of 'novo' (young) is also 'velho' (old/elderly).

rules.rule: Common verb opposites — pairs that express opposite actions. These are essential for daily conversation.
rules.rulePt: Opostos comuns de verbos — pares que expressam ações opostas. São essenciais para a conversa do dia a dia.

examples.en: to go up → to go down
to fall asleep → to wake up

exceptions: 'Acabar' can also mean 'to end up' — 'Acabei por concordar' (I ended up agreeing). Context determines meaning.
In EP, 'começar' is more common than 'iniciar' in everyday speech, though both are correct.

rules.rule: Common adverb and preposition opposites. These come up constantly in giving directions, describing time, and comparing things.
rules.rulePt: Opostos comuns de advérbios e preposições. Aparecem constantemente ao dar direções, descrever tempo e comparar coisas.
exceptions: In EP, 'depressa' is the standard word for quickly/fast. Brazilian Portuguese uses 'rápido' as an adverb more often. In EP, 'rápido' is primarily an adjective.

rules.rule: Common noun opposites — pairs that represent opposite concepts. Many of these involve gender changes or completely different words.
rules.rulePt: Opostos comuns de nomes — pares que representam conceitos opostos. Muitos envolvem mudanças de género ou palavras completamente diferentes.
exceptions: Notice how some pairs change gender: 'o dia' (m) → 'a noite' (f), 'o início' (m) → 'o fim' (m). There is no pattern — memorise the genders with each word.

tips: When you see an unfamiliar word starting with 'in-', 'im-', 'des-', 'il-', or 'ir-', try removing the prefix. If the remaining word makes sense, you have found the opposite.
The prefix 'des-' is by far the most versatile. If you are unsure how to say the opposite of a verb, try adding 'des-' — it works more often than you think.
Many opposite pairs in Portuguese mirror English exactly: possível/impossível, legal/ilegal, regular/irregular. Your English knowledge is a huge advantage here.
Be careful with 'fechar' and 'acabar'. They sound similar to beginners but mean very different things: fechar = to close (a door, a shop), acabar = to finish/end.
tipsPt: Quando vês uma palavra desconhecida que começa com 'in-', 'im-', 'des-', 'il-' ou 'ir-', tenta remover o prefixo. Se a palavra restante fizer sentido, encontraste o oposto.
O prefixo 'des-' é de longe o mais versátil. Se não sabes como dizer o oposto de um verbo, tenta acrescentar 'des-' — funciona mais vezes do que pensas.
Muitos pares de opostos em português são iguais ao inglês: possível/impossível, legal/ilegal, regular/irregular. O teu conhecimento de inglês é uma grande vantagem.
Tem cuidado com 'fechar' e 'acabar'. Parecem semelhantes para iniciantes mas significam coisas muito diferentes: fechar = to close (uma porta, uma loja), acabar = to finish/end.

questions.explanation: Before 'p', the prefix 'in-' changes to 'im-'. So possível → impossível.
questions.exampleTranslation: It's impossible to arrive on time.

questions.explanation: 'Feliz' starts with 'f', so the standard prefix 'in-' applies: infeliz.
questions.exampleSentence: Ele parece infeliz com a decisão.
questions.exampleTranslation: He seems unhappy with the decision.

questions.explanation: Before words starting with 'l', the prefix becomes 'il-'. Legal → ilegal.
questions.exampleTranslation: Parking here is illegal.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'to turn off' in Portuguese?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'to turn off' em português?
questions.explanation: 'Ligar' means to turn on / connect. Adding 'des-' reverses it: desligar = to turn off / disconnect.
questions.exampleSentence: Podes desligar a luz, por favor?
questions.exampleTranslation: Can you turn off the light, please?

questions.explanation: 'Abrir' (to open) and 'fechar' (to close) are a common opposite pair — no prefix pattern, just different words.
questions.exampleTranslation: Close the door, please.

questions.explanation: Comprar (to buy) → vender (to sell). These are separate words, not a prefix pattern.
questions.exampleTranslation: I want to sell my car.

questions.explanation: Sempre (always) → nunca (never). A fundamental adverb pair.
questions.exampleTranslation: I have never been to the Algarve.

questions.questionText: Which sentence correctly uses a prefix opposite?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual frase usa corretamente um oposto com prefixo?
questions.explanation: 'Paciente' starts with 'p', so the prefix 'in-' changes to 'im-': impaciente.
questions.exampleTranslation: Don't be so impatient!

questions.explanation: Before words starting with 'r', the prefix becomes 'ir-'. Responsável → irresponsável.
questions.exampleTranslation: It was an irresponsible attitude.

questions.explanation: The prefix 'des-' reverses the verb: aparecer (to appear) → desaparecer (to disappear).
questions.exampleSentence: O gato desapareceu há dois dias.
questions.exampleTranslation: The cat disappeared two days ago.

questions.explanation: Quente (hot) → frio (cold). This is a word pair, not a prefix opposite. 'Gelado' means frozen/icy, which is more extreme than 'frio'.
questions.exampleTranslation: It's very cold today.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'the exit' in Portuguese?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'the exit' em português?
questions.explanation: Entrada (entrance) → saída (exit). Both are feminine nouns.
questions.exampleTranslation: The exit is over there.

questions.explanation: Lembrar (to remember) → esquecer (to forget). These are separate words. 'Recordar' is a synonym of 'lembrar', not its opposite.
questions.exampleTranslation: I forgot the keys at home.

questions.explanation: Cedo (early) → tarde (late). Note: 'tarde' also means 'afternoon' as a noun (a tarde).
questions.exampleTranslation: We arrived late to the restaurant.

questions.questionText: If 'cobrir' means 'to cover', what does 'descobrir' mean?
questions.questionTextPt: Se 'cobrir' significa 'to cover', o que significa 'descobrir'?
questions.explanation: The prefix 'des-' reverses the meaning: cobrir (to cover) → descobrir (to uncover / discover). Portugal's Age of Discovery is 'a Era dos Descobrimentos'.
questions.exampleSentence: Descobri um restaurante ótimo perto de casa.
questions.exampleTranslation: I discovered a great restaurant near home.

questions.explanation: Dentro (inside) → fora (outside). Used constantly in daily life: 'cá dentro' (in here), 'lá fora' (out there).
questions.exampleSentence: As crianças estão lá fora a brincar.
questions.exampleTranslation: The children are outside playing.

questions.explanation: 'Organizado' uses the prefix 'des-' for its opposite: desorganizado. The 'des-' prefix is used with many adjectives derived from verbs.
questions.exampleSentence: O escritório dele é muito desorganizado.
questions.exampleTranslation: His office is very disorganised.

questions.explanation: Subir (to go up) → descer (to go down). 'Baixar' means to lower (e.g. volume), and 'cair' means to fall — different meanings.
questions.exampleTranslation: You have to go down the stairs.

questions.explanation: Perto (near) → longe (far). 'Perto de' and 'longe de' are used with 'de' to say near/far from something.
questions.exampleTranslation: Is the beach far from here?

questions.questionText: You see the word 'ilegível' for the first time. Based on prefix rules, what does it mean?
questions.questionTextPt: Vês a palavra 'ilegível' pela primeira vez. Com base nas regras de prefixos, o que significa?
questions.explanation: The prefix 'il-' is used before 'l'. Remove it and you get 'legível' (legible/readable). So 'ilegível' = illegible/unreadable. This is the power of knowing prefix rules!
questions.exampleTranslation: His handwriting is illegible.

plural-formation.title: Plural Formation
plural-formation.summary: How to make Portuguese nouns and adjectives plural — from simple -s to the tricky -ão patterns.
plural-formation.intro:
Portuguese plurals are mostly straightforward: add -s to most words. But some endings require special treatment. Words ending in consonants like -r, -s, -z add -es. Words ending in -l change to -is. And the infamous -ão ending has three possible plural forms — -ões, -ães, or -ãos — that you'll need to learn case by case.

The good news: the -s rule covers the majority of words, and the special patterns become intuitive with practice.

rules.rule: Most nouns and adjectives: add -s. This covers words ending in a vowel (-a, -o, -e).
rules.rulePt: A maioria dos nomes e adjetivos: adicionar -s.

rules.rule: Words ending in -r, -s, -z: add -es.
rules.rulePt: Palavras terminadas em -r, -s, -z: adicionar -es.

rules.rule: Words ending in -al, -el, -ol, -ul: drop the -l and add -is.
rules.rulePt: Palavras terminadas em -al, -el, -ol, -ul: trocar -l por -is.

rules.rule: Words ending in -ão: three patterns. Most common: -ões. Some: -ães. Few: -ãos.
rules.rulePt: Palavras terminadas em -ão: três padrões — -ões (mais comum), -ães, -ãos.

rules.rule: Words ending in -m: change -m to -ns.
rules.rulePt: Palavras terminadas em -m: trocar -m por -ns.

tips: The -s rule covers about 80% of Portuguese words. When in doubt, try adding -s first.
For -ão words, the -ões pattern is the most common (coração → corações, avião → aviões). Learn -ães and -ãos as exceptions.
Words ending in -il have two patterns: stressed -il → -is (barril → barris), unstressed -il → -eis (fácil → fáceis).
Some words don't change in the plural: lápis → lápis, ónibus → ónibus. These are words already ending in -s with the stress not on the last syllable.
tipsPt: A regra do -s cobre cerca de 80% das palavras. Em caso de dúvida, tenta adicionar -s.
Para palavras em -ão, o padrão -ões é o mais comum.
Algumas palavras não mudam no plural: lápis → lápis.

questions.explanation: Gato ends in a vowel, so just add -s: gatos.
Words ending in -r add -es: flor → flores.
Words ending in -al drop the -l and add -is: animal → animais.
Limão follows the most common -ão pattern: -ões → limões.
Words ending in -m change -m to -ns: homem → homens.
Mão is one of the few -ão words that simply adds -s: mãos.
Words ending in -el drop the -l and add -is, with an accent: papel → papéis.
Words ending in -s (stressed) add -es: país → países.
Pão follows the -ães pattern: pão → pães.
Words ending in -z add -es: rapaz → rapazes.
Words ending in -ul drop the -l and add -is: azul → azuis.
Words ending in -m change -m to -ns: viagem → viagens.

questions.questionText: Which -ão plural pattern is the MOST common?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual padrão de plural em -ão é o MAIS comum?
questions.explanation: The -ões pattern is by far the most common (coração → corações, limão → limões, etc.).
Words ending in -al drop the -l and add -is: jornal → jornais.
Cão follows the -ães pattern: cão → cães (like pão → pães).

adjective-agreement.title: Adjective Agreement
adjective-agreement.summary: Portuguese adjectives change form to match the gender and number of the noun they describe.
adjective-agreement.intro:
Unlike English, Portuguese adjectives must agree with their noun in both gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural). Most adjectives ending in -o have a feminine form in -a: bonito/bonita. Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant typically don't change for gender.

Another key difference: most Portuguese adjectives come AFTER the noun (um carro vermelho, not *um vermelho carro), though a few common ones like bom, mau, grande can come before.

rules.rule: Gender agreement: adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine. Adjectives ending in -e or consonant usually stay the same.
rules.rulePt: Concordância de género: adjetivos em -o mudam para -a no feminino. Em -e ou consoante geralmente não mudam.

rules.rule: Number agreement: add -s to make adjectives plural, following the same plural rules as nouns.
rules.rulePt: Concordância de número: adicionar -s para o plural, seguindo as mesmas regras dos nomes.

rules.rule: Position: most adjectives come AFTER the noun in Portuguese.
rules.rulePt: Posição: a maioria dos adjetivos vem DEPOIS do nome.

rules.rule: Exceptions: some adjectives change meaning based on position. A few common adjectives (bom, mau, primeiro, último) typically come before the noun.
rules.rulePt: Exceções: alguns adjetivos mudam de significado conforme a posição. Bom, mau, primeiro, último vêm antes do nome.

examples.pt: um grande homem (a great man) vs um homem grande (a big man)

tips: When a group contains both masculine and feminine nouns, the adjective takes the masculine plural form: 'O João e a Maria são altos.'
Adjectives ending in -or usually add -a for feminine: trabalhador → trabalhadora. Exception: some comparative forms (maior, menor, melhor, pior) don't change.
Nationality adjectives follow normal agreement: português/portuguesa, espanhol/espanhola, inglês/inglesa.
Colors follow agreement rules: vermelho/vermelha, but some compound colors don't change: cor-de-rosa, cor-de-laranja.
tipsPt: Num grupo misto, o adjetivo fica no masculino plural: 'O João e a Maria são altos.'
Adjetivos de nacionalidade seguem a concordância normal: português/portuguesa.
Algumas cores compostas não mudam: cor-de-rosa, cor-de-laranja.

questions.questionText: What is the feminine form of 'bonito'?
questions.explanation: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine: bonito → bonita.

questions.questionText: Where does the adjective go in 'a ___ house (beautiful)'?
questions.questionTextPt: Onde fica o adjetivo em 'a casa ___'?
questions.explanation: Most adjectives come AFTER the noun: uma casa bonita.
Add -s to adjectives ending in -e: grande → grandes.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'a red car' in Portuguese?
questions.explanation: The adjective comes after the noun, and 'carro' is masculine: um carro vermelho.
When 'grande' comes BEFORE the noun, it means 'great'. After the noun (um homem grande), it means 'big'.
Adjectives ending in a consonant like -z typically don't change for gender: ele é feliz / ela é feliz.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'beautiful houses' (feminine plural)?
questions.explanation: Casa is feminine, plural → casas. Bonito → bonita (f.) → bonitas (f. pl.).

questions.questionText: Which adjective typically comes BEFORE the noun?
questions.questionTextPt: Que adjetivo normalmente vem ANTES do nome?
questions.explanation: Bom (good) is one of the few adjectives that typically comes before the noun: um bom livro.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'a nice person (female)'?
questions.explanation: Pessoa is feminine, so the adjective agrees: simpático → simpática. It goes after the noun.
Adjectives ending in -or add -a for feminine: trabalhador → trabalhadora.

questions.questionText: 'O João e a Maria são ___' (tall). Which form?
questions.questionTextPt: 'O João e a Maria são ___' (altos). Que forma?
questions.explanation: Mixed groups use the masculine plural: altos.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'the first time'?
questions.explanation: Primeiro/primeira comes BEFORE the noun: a primeira vez.

questions.questionText: What is the correct form: 'uma mulher ___' (Portuguese)?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a forma correta: 'uma mulher ___'?
questions.explanation: Mulher is feminine, so: português → portuguesa.
Interessante ends in -e, so the plural is interessantes. It goes after the noun.
Compound colors like cor-de-rosa don't change for gender or number.

adverbs.title: Adverbs
adverbs.summary: Words that modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs — quickly, always, very, here.
adverbs.intro:
Portuguese adverbs work similarly to English ones: they modify verbs (ele fala rapidamente — he speaks quickly), adjectives (muito bonito — very beautiful), or other adverbs (bastante bem — quite well).

The key difference from adjectives: adverbs NEVER change for gender or number. 'Rapidamente' stays 'rapidamente' whether you're talking about him, her, or them.

rules.rule: Adverbs of manner: add -mente to the feminine form of the adjective. If the adjective has no feminine form, add -mente directly.
rules.rulePt: Advérbios de modo: adicionar -mente ao feminino do adjetivo.

rules.rule: Adverbs of time: words that tell you WHEN something happens.
rules.rulePt: Advérbios de tempo: dizem QUANDO algo acontece.

examples.en: Tomorrow I'm going to the doctor.

rules.rule: Adverbs of place: words that tell you WHERE something is or happens.
rules.rulePt: Advérbios de lugar: dizem ONDE algo é ou acontece.

rules.rule: Adverbs of quantity: words that tell you HOW MUCH.
rules.rulePt: Advérbios de quantidade: dizem QUANTO.

tips: Unlike adjectives, adverbs NEVER change for gender or number: 'Ela fala rapidamente' (not *rapidamenta).
When two -mente adverbs appear together, only the last one keeps -mente: 'Ele fala clara e rapidamente' (not *claramente e rapidamente).
Muito can be an adverb (invariable: 'Ele come muito') or an adjective (variable: 'Muitos livros'). Context tells you which.
The EP adverbs of place have nuances: aqui (right here, near me), aí (there, near you), ali (over there, visible), lá (far away), cá (general 'here' area).
tipsPt: Advérbios NUNCA mudam de género ou número: 'Ela fala rapidamente'.
Com dois advérbios em -mente seguidos, só o último mantém -mente: 'clara e rapidamente'.
Muito pode ser advérbio (invariável) ou adjetivo (variável).

questions.questionText: How do you form the adverb from 'rápida' (quick, f.)?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se forma o advérbio de 'rápida'?
questions.explanation: Add -mente to the feminine form: rápida + -mente = rapidamente (no accent on the a).

questions.questionText: Which of these is an adverb of time?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual destes é um advérbio de tempo?
questions.explanation: Amanhã (tomorrow) tells you WHEN — it's an adverb of time.

questions.questionText: Do adverbs change for gender in Portuguese?
questions.questionTextPt: Os advérbios mudam de género em português?
questions.explanation: Adverbs NEVER change for gender or number. 'Rapidamente' is always 'rapidamente'.

questions.questionText: What does 'Ele nunca chega a horas' mean?
questions.questionTextPt: O que significa 'Ele nunca chega a horas'?
questions.explanation: Nunca means 'never'. A horas means 'on time'.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'She lives far away'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'She lives far away'?
questions.explanation: Longe means 'far away'. It's an adverb of place.

questions.questionText: What is the adverb form of 'feliz'?
questions.explanation: Feliz has no separate feminine form, so add -mente directly: felizmente.

questions.questionText: In EP, what's the difference between 'aqui' and 'cá'?
questions.questionTextPt: Em PE, qual é a diferença entre 'aqui' e 'cá'?
options: They mean exactly the same thing
Aqui = a specific spot here; cá = general area here
questions.correctAnswer: Aqui = a specific spot here; cá = general area here
questions.explanation: Aqui points to a precise spot (right here). Cá refers to a general area (around here, over here).

questions.questionText: 'Ele come muito' — is 'muito' an adverb or adjective here?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Ele come muito' — 'muito' é advérbio ou adjetivo?
questions.explanation: Here 'muito' modifies the verb 'come' (how much he eats), so it's an adverb. As an adverb, it doesn't change.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'I've already eaten'?
questions.explanation: Já means 'already'. Já comi = I've already eaten.

questions.questionText: What is the adverb from 'lenta' (slow, f.)?
questions.explanation: Add -mente to the feminine form: lenta + -mente = lentamente.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'He works too much'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'He works too much'?
questions.explanation: Demais means 'too much' (excess). Bastante means 'quite/enough'.

questions.questionText: When two -mente adverbs are together, what happens?
questions.questionTextPt: Quando dois advérbios em -mente estão juntos, o que acontece?
questions.explanation: In Portuguese, only the last adverb keeps -mente: 'clara e rapidamente' (not *claramente e rapidamente).

imperative.title: Imperative (Commands)
imperative.summary: How to give orders, instructions, and make requests in Portuguese — from polite to direct.
imperative.intro:
The imperative mood is how you tell someone to do something: 'Sit down!', 'Please wait', 'Don't touch that'. Portuguese has different imperative forms depending on who you're addressing (tu vs você) and whether the command is affirmative or negative.

In European Portuguese, the tu form is far more common in daily life than the você form. The tu imperative is simple: it's the same as the 3rd person singular present tense (ele/ela form).

rules.rule: Tu form (informal, affirmative): use the 3rd person singular present tense — drop the -s from the tu form.
rules.rulePt: Forma tu (informal, afirmativo): usar a 3.ª pessoa do singular do presente.

rules.rule: Você form (formal, affirmative): use the present subjunctive.
rules.rulePt: Forma você (formal, afirmativo): usar o presente do conjuntivo.

rules.rule: Negative commands: use não + present subjunctive for both tu and você.
rules.rulePt: Imperativo negativo: usar não + presente do conjuntivo para tu e você.

rules.rule: Common irregular tu imperatives: sê (ser), está (estar), tem (ter), vai (ir), diz (dizer), faz (fazer), vem (vir).
rules.rulePt: Imperativos irregulares comuns (tu): sê, está, tem, vai, diz, faz, vem.

tips: In EP, the tu imperative is used much more than in BR. Even in shops and restaurants, people use it with strangers of similar age.
Adding 'por favor' softens any command: 'Espera, por favor' (Wait, please).
The nós imperative exists too — it's the same as the present subjunctive: 'Falemos!' (Let's speak!). But 'Vamos falar!' is more common in speech.
With reflexive verbs in the affirmative, the pronoun attaches: 'Senta-te!' (Sit down!). In the negative: 'Não te sentes!'
tipsPt: Em PE, o imperativo com tu é muito mais usado do que com você.
Adicionar 'por favor' suaviza qualquer ordem.
Com verbos reflexivos no afirmativo, o pronome liga-se: 'Senta-te!'

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Speak!' (informal, to a friend)?
questions.explanation: The tu imperative uses the 3rd person present: ele fala → Fala!

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Speak!' (formal, to a stranger)?
questions.explanation: The você imperative uses the present subjunctive: Fale!

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Don't eat that!' (tu)?
questions.explanation: Negative tu commands use não + present subjunctive: não comas.

questions.questionText: What is the tu imperative of 'ir'?
questions.explanation: Ir is irregular in the imperative: Vai! (Go!)
Vir has the irregular imperative: Vem! (Come!)

questions.questionText: What form does the negative imperative use for both tu and você?
questions.questionTextPt: Que forma usa o imperativo negativo para tu e você?
questions.explanation: Negative commands always use não + present subjunctive, regardless of tu or você.

questions.questionText: 'Faz o que te digo!' — which verb is in the imperative?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Faz o que te digo!' — qual verbo está no imperativo?
questions.explanation: Faz is the tu imperative of fazer. Digo is present indicative (I say).

questions.questionText: How do you make a tu command softer?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se suaviza uma ordem com tu?
questions.explanation: Adding 'por favor' makes any command more polite: 'Espera, por favor.'

questions.questionText: What is the tu imperative of 'abrir'?
questions.explanation: Regular -IR verbs: ele abre → Abre! (the tu imperative matches the 3rd person present).

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Don't open the door!' (tu)?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Não abras a porta!' (tu)?
questions.explanation: Negative tu: não + subjunctive → Não abras!

questions.questionText: What is the tu imperative of 'dizer'?
questions.explanation: Dizer is irregular: the tu imperative is Diz! (Tell! / Say!)

questions.questionText: Which is the correct formal command for 'Wait'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é a forma formal correta para 'Espere'?
questions.explanation: The você imperative uses the present subjunctive: Espere!

questions.questionText: How do you say 'Tell the truth!' (tu)?
questions.explanation: The tu imperative of dizer is Diz!

questions.questionText: What is the tu imperative of 'ser'?
questions.explanation: Ser has the irregular tu imperative: Sê! (Be!). E.g., 'Sê forte!' (Be strong!)

questions.questionText: 'Não fales tão alto!' — who is this addressed to?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Não fales tão alto!' — a quem se dirige?
questions.explanation: 'Fales' is the tu form of the present subjunctive, used in negative tu commands.

estar-a-infinitive.title: Estar a + Infinitive (Progressive)
estar-a-infinitive.summary: The Portuguese equivalent of English '-ing' — how to say what's happening right now.
estar-a-infinitive.intro:
When you want to say what's happening right now — 'I'm eating', 'She's reading', 'They're working' — European Portuguese uses estar a + infinitive. This is one of the key differences between EP and Brazilian Portuguese, which uses the gerund (-ando/-endo/-indo) instead.

The construction is simple: conjugate estar in any tense, add 'a', then the infinitive of the main verb. It works with every tense of estar to express ongoing actions at different times.

rules.rule: Formation: conjugate estar + a + infinitive. This expresses an action happening right now.
rules.rulePt: Formação: conjugar estar + a + infinitivo. Exprime uma ação a acontecer agora.

examples.pt: Ela está a ler um livro.

rules.rule: EP vs BR: European Portuguese uses 'estar a + infinitive'. Brazilian Portuguese uses 'estar + gerund (-ando/-endo/-indo)'.
rules.rulePt: PE vs PB: PE usa 'estar a + infinitivo'. PB usa 'estar + gerúndio'.

examples.pt: EP: Estou a falar. / BR: Estou falando.
examples.en: I'm speaking. (two ways to say it)

examples.pt: EP: Está a chover. / BR: Está chovendo.
EP: Estamos a aprender. / BR: Estamos aprendendo.

rules.rule: Works with any tense of estar: imperfect (ongoing in the past), future, conditional, etc.
rules.rulePt: Funciona com qualquer tempo de estar: imperfeito, futuro, condicional, etc.

examples.en: It was raining when I left.

examples.pt: Amanhã a esta hora vou estar a trabalhar.
examples.en: Tomorrow at this time I'll be working.

examples.pt: Estávamos a jantar quando ele chegou.
examples.en: We were having dinner when he arrived.

tips: This is the standard EP way to express ongoing actions. If you use the gerund (-ando/-endo/-indo) in Portugal, people will understand but it sounds Brazilian.
Don't use 'estar a' for habitual actions. 'I work every day' = 'Trabalho todos os dias' (present simple), NOT *'Estou a trabalhar todos os dias'.
Negation is simple: 'Não estou a perceber.' (I'm not understanding.)
Some verbs work well with estar a, others don't. State verbs like saber, gostar, querer rarely use this construction.
tipsPt: Esta é a forma padrão do PE para ações em curso. O gerúndio soa brasileiro em Portugal.
Não usar 'estar a' para ações habituais.
Negação: 'Não estou a perceber.'

questions.questionText: How do you say 'I'm eating' in EP?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'I'm eating' em PE?
questions.explanation: EP uses estar a + infinitive: Estou a comer.

questions.questionText: What is the EP equivalent of 'Estou falando'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é o equivalente em PE de 'Estou falando'?
questions.explanation: 'Estou falando' is BR. In EP: 'Estou a falar.'

questions.questionText: How do you say 'It was raining' in EP?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'It was raining' em PE?
questions.explanation: Use imperfect of estar + a + infinitive: Estava a chover.

questions.questionText: Which is correct EP: 'She's reading a book'?
questions.questionTextPt: Qual é correto em PE: 'She's reading a book'?
options: Ela está a ler um livro.
Ela é a ler um livro.
questions.correctAnswer: Ela está a ler um livro.
questions.explanation: EP: estar (conjugated) + a + infinitive → Ela está a ler.
Estar a + infinitive expresses what's happening right now.

questions.questionText: Can you use 'estar a' with the imperfect tense?
questions.questionTextPt: Pode-se usar 'estar a' com o imperfeito?
questions.explanation: 'Estava a chover' (It was raining) — estar a works with any tense of estar.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'We're learning Portuguese' in EP?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'We're learning Portuguese' em PE?
questions.explanation: EP: Estamos a aprender. (BR would be: Estamos aprendendo.)

questions.questionText: How do you negate 'Estou a perceber'?
questions.explanation: Place 'não' before estar: Não estou a perceber.

options: Estou a gostar de chocolate. (I'm liking chocolate.)
Estou a trabalhar agora. (I'm working now.)
Estou a saber a resposta. (I'm knowing the answer.)
Estou a querer ir. (I'm wanting to go.)
questions.correctAnswer: Estou a trabalhar agora. (I'm working now.)
questions.explanation: Estar a works best with action verbs. State verbs (gostar, saber, querer) don't normally use this construction.

questions.questionText: Should you use 'estar a' for habitual actions?
questions.questionTextPt: Deve usar-se 'estar a' para ações habituais?
questions.explanation: 'I work every day' = 'Trabalho todos os dias' (present simple), not *'Estou a trabalhar todos os dias'.

questions.questionText: 'Estávamos a jantar quando ele chegou' — what tense is estar in?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Estávamos a jantar quando ele chegou' — em que tempo está 'estar'?
questions.explanation: Estávamos is the imperfect of estar, expressing an ongoing past action interrupted by another event.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'They're playing football' in EP?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'They're playing football' em PE?
questions.explanation: EP: Eles estão a jogar. (BR: Eles estão jogando.)

ter-de-infinitive.title: Ter de + Infinitive (Obligation)
ter-de-infinitive.summary: Expressing obligation and necessity — 'I have to', 'you must', 'we need to'.
ter-de-infinitive.intro:
When you need to express obligation in Portuguese — 'I have to study', 'you must leave', 'we need to go' — the most common construction is ter de + infinitive. It's the Portuguese equivalent of English 'have to'.

You'll also encounter ter que (with 'que' instead of 'de'), which is equally common in speech but slightly less standard in formal writing. Both are perfectly correct in everyday EP.

rules.rule: Formation: conjugate ter + de + infinitive. 'Tenho de estudar' = I have to study.
rules.rulePt: Formação: conjugar ter + de + infinitivo. 'Tenho de estudar' = Tenho de estudar.

examples.pt: Tenho de estudar para o exame.
examples.en: I have to study for the exam.
You have to go to the doctor.

rules.rule: Ter de vs ter que: both are correct in EP. 'Ter de' is slightly more standard; 'ter que' is very common in speech.
rules.rulePt: Ter de vs ter que: ambos são corretos em PE. 'Ter de' é ligeiramente mais formal.

examples.pt: Tenho de ir. / Tenho que ir.
Tens de fazer isso. / Tens que fazer isso.
examples.en: You have to do that. (both correct)

rules.rule: Compare: dever (should/moral obligation), precisar de (need), ter de (have to/requirement).
rules.rulePt: Comparar: dever (dever moral), precisar de (necessidade), ter de (obrigação/requisito).

examples.pt: Devo estudar. (I should study — moral duty)
Preciso de estudar. (I need to study — personal need)
Tenho de estudar. (I have to study — external requirement)
É preciso estudar. (One must study — impersonal)

tips: Ter de is the most versatile — it works for any obligation, from mild ('Tenho de lavar a loiça') to strong ('Tens de ir ao hospital agora!').
Dever expresses moral duty or advice: 'Deves ser honesto' (You should be honest). It's softer than ter de.
Precisar de expresses personal need: 'Preciso de dormir' (I need to sleep). It's about YOUR need, not an external rule.
In the negative, 'não tens de' means 'you don't have to' (no obligation), while 'não deves' means 'you shouldn't' (advice against).
tipsPt: Ter de é o mais versátil — funciona para qualquer obrigação.
Dever exprime dever moral ou conselho.
Precisar de exprime necessidade pessoal.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'I have to study' using ter de?
questions.explanation: Ter de + infinitive: Tenho de estudar.

questions.questionText: Is 'Tenho que ir' correct in EP?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Tenho que ir' é correto em PE?
questions.explanation: Both 'ter de' and 'ter que' are correct in EP. 'Ter de' is slightly more standard.

questions.questionText: 'Devo estudar' expresses what kind of obligation?
questions.questionTextPt: 'Devo estudar' exprime que tipo de obrigação?
questions.explanation: Dever expresses moral obligation or advice (should), not hard requirement (have to).

questions.questionText: How do you say 'You have to go to the doctor'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Tens de ir ao médico'?
questions.explanation: Ter de + infinitive: Tens de ir.
Precisar de expresses personal need/necessity.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'We have to leave now'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Temos de sair agora'?
questions.explanation: Ter de + infinitive for obligation: Temos de sair agora.

options: You don't have to do that
questions.correctAnswer: You don't have to do that
questions.explanation: 'Não tens de' = you don't have to (no obligation). 'Não deves' = you shouldn't (advice against).

questions.questionTextPt: Qual exprime a obrigação MAIS forte?
questions.explanation: Tenho de = I have to (strongest obligation). Devo = I should. Preciso de = I need to. Quero = I want to.

questions.questionText: How do you say 'They have to work tomorrow'?
questions.questionTextPt: Como se diz 'Eles têm de trabalhar amanhã'?
questions.explanation: Eles/elas use 'têm' (with accent). Ter de + infinitive: têm de trabalhar.
In EP: precisar DE + infinitive. 'Preciso de estudar.' (In BR, the 'de' is often dropped.)

por-vs-para.title: Por vs Para
por-vs-para.summary: Two prepositions that both translate as 'for' — when to use which, and why it matters.
por-vs-para.intro:
Both por and para can translate as 'for' in English, which makes them one of the trickiest distinctions for learners. The key insight: para points FORWARD (destination, purpose, recipient), while por looks BACK or THROUGH (cause, exchange, route).

Think of para as an arrow pointing to the future or a target: 'Where is it going? Who is it for? What is it for?' And por as explaining the reason or path: 'Why? In exchange for what? Through where?'

rules.rule: Para = destination, purpose, recipient. Points forward to a goal or target.
rules.rulePt: Para = destino, finalidade, destinatário. Aponta para um objetivo.

rules.rule: Por = cause, exchange, movement through. Looks back at the reason or path.
rules.rulePt: Por = causa, troca, passagem. Olha para a razão ou caminho.

examples.en: I paid ten euros for this. (exchange)
I did that out of love. (motivation)

rules.rule: Para = deadline or time limit. Por = duration or frequency (per).
rules.rulePt: Para = prazo. Por = duração ou frequência (por).

rules.rule: Por = on behalf of, by (agent in passive). Para = in order to, intended for.
rules.rulePt: Por = em nome de, por (agente na passiva). Para = a fim de, destinado a.

examples.en: I did this for you / on your behalf.
I made this for you. (intended recipient)

examples.pt: O livro foi escrito por ela.
examples.en: The book was written by her. (passive agent)

tips: Quick test: if you can replace 'for' with 'in order to', use para. If you can replace it with 'because of', use por.
'Fiz isto por ti' (I did this for your sake/because of you) vs 'Fiz isto para ti' (I made this for you/to give to you) — subtle but important!
Por contracts with articles: por + o = pelo, por + a = pela, por + os = pelos, por + as = pelas.
Para never contracts with articles. 'Para o' stays 'para o' (or 'pro' in very informal speech).
When in doubt about movement: para = destination (going TO), por = passing through.
tipsPt: Teste rápido: se podes substituir 'for' por 'in order to', usa para. Se podes substituir por 'because of', usa por.
Por contrai com artigos: pelo, pela, pelos, pelas.
Para não contrai com artigos na norma padrão.

questions.explanation: Para indicates destination: Vou para Lisboa (I'm going to Lisbon).
Por indicates cause/reason: Obrigado por tudo (Thank you for everything).

questions.questionText: 'Isto é ___ ti.' (This is for you — a gift)
questions.explanation: Para indicates recipient: Isto é para ti.
Por indicates exchange: I paid ten euros FOR (in exchange for) this.
